text: The surgeon uses cold scissors to dissect and free the small bowel adhered to the right adnexa and the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection, identifying the ureter before isolating the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) proximal to the left colic artery. A tunnel is created, and the vessel is divided using a vessel sealer. The surgeon continues the dissection from the medial space until visibility is limited, then places a sponge and transitions to the lateral dissection.

The surgeon enters the medially dissected plane, revealing the sponge placed earlier. The mesentery is lifted off the kidney as the dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure. The surgeon switches to the vessel sealer to continue dissecting the plane between the splenic flexure and the left kidney.

The patient remains in a steep Trendelenburg position with a slight left side elevation. The surgeon uses an inferior lateral approach to mobilize the splenic flexure, revealing the inferior border of the pancreas. The dissection continues along the base of the pancreas, followed by lateral dissection to separate diseased tissue from the uterus and left pelvic sidewall. The surgeon then dissects down from the dome of the bladder and uterus, exposing a granulation-filled cavity.

The surgeon has freed the colon and is clearing the rectal margin of its mesentery. The proximal margin is also being cleared, extending up to the valve wall. The surgeon checks perfusion, marking with cautery both proximally and distally to ensure proper transection. The upper rectum is then divided using scissors, chosen for reduced thermal spread compared to a vessel sealer.

The surgeon begins to chop the specimen into smaller pieces, starting by removing the mesentery without using an Alexis wound protector. A ring clamp is used to extract the mesentery, followed by morseling the specimen into smaller portions. The surgeon alternates between sitting and standing to remove each piece individually from below, using scissors when the vessel sealer is insufficient for cutting through dense tissue.
The surgeon removes the final two pieces of the diseased colon, ensuring each transected piece is extracted individually to prevent any from flipping into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon performs a sewn two-layer anastomosis on the transected bowel wall, beginning with the back outer row of seromuscular bites. The procedure involves using 3-0 nine-inch V-Lux sutures, transitioning from the inner back row to the inner anterior row, and imbricating the lip with canal sutures. The surgeon adjusts the technique based on suture angles, terminating and finishing stitches as needed.

The surgeon ties the two layers of the anastomosis together, sewing the posterior outer row around anteriorly. A leak test is performed using a 19mm rigid scope through the anastomosis, showing no bubbles or bleeding, indicating a successful seal. The surgical area is cleaned up, concluding the procedure.
